name: Study 4: An In-Between Move To Attack

comments.text:
For the last study of this chapter, we'll go over a type of intermezzo known as the desperado.   

This is a game played In 1935 in Moscow, between Jose Raul Capablanca and Grigory Levenfish. 

This game features a so-called "desperado" move. A desperado occurs when you'll lose a piece, so you use that piece to capture as much material as possible.

Let's check it out!
https://www.chessgames.com/perl/chessgame?gid=1090631
Black added support to their b5-pawn.
Black put pressure on the d4-pawn.
And checkmate.
Capablanca started out with the d-pawn.

And when White recaptures, Black is a pawn up. 

This was not good for Capablanca.
...a discovered check and Qh7# next.
White threatened Nxf7+ and Qh7#.
The only move.
Black attacked the bishop and saved the h7-pawn.
White got their king into safety.
Black has to recapture.
...a thematic sacrifice would arise once again.
White developed their second knight to f3.
Black finally recaptured the knight on b5.
For example, after this one...

Black offered a rook trade, which wasn't the best way to proceed.

The rook was actually great on the 5th rank.
White added support to the d4-pawn and opened up their light-squared bishop.
Black traded in the center, counter-attacking the knight on c3.
Forced.

And since the bishop left the e7-square, the knight could be pinned now.

The threat is Bxf6 to undermine the h-pawn.
Since White has made a move with their bishop already, Black now traded on c4 and forced White to move yet again.
Black expanded on the queenside with a tempo.
And after this capture, Black has a series of captures in which he comes out on top.

Capablanca missed a checkmate in 7 moves, starting with Nxh6+! 

Of course, easy to say when analyzing the game, but let's check it out nonetheless!
But Black has a pin!
White developed a piece.
The captures continue.
It was important to keep the rook on the fifth rank for protection and continue by developing the bishop instead.
Let's see what would happen if Black accepted the sacrifice.
The best move was ...h5, to take away the g4-square from White's knight.
White got their queen out to a nice square, while attacking the rook on a8.
After ...Bxf6...
By pushing the e-pawn to e5, White gained even more space and opened up their light-squared bishop, while also attacking the opponent's knight.
Levenfish responded symmetrically.
Check!
Levenfish went ...Rh8 and allowed the queen to infiltrate even further.
And The Queen's Gambit appeared!
Let's check out a fun line, starting with Rxc8, an exchange sacrifice!
Because White's knight is trapped.
And mate.
Black has opened their rook along the fifth rank and attacked White's knight.
This is also forced.
Checkmate!
Black prepared to castle.
Only legal move.
Maybe capturing the pawn on d4 comes to mind...
Perhaps pushing d5 would be better — creating counterplay against Black's king which isn't castled yet.
Black recaptures.
Black recaptures.
This just prolongs the game.
Black added yet another supporter to the d5-pawn.

Black protected their b5-pawn while getting out of the attack.

Capablanca correctly evaluated that Nc6 wasn't the best for him.

And here came a Desperado move! 

Since White is already attacking Black's knight on f6, capturing a pawn was a possibility here! 

Essentially going for activity, instead of passively retreating the knight and letting Black expand on the queenside.
A check that forced the king to the h8-square.
Black recaptures.
The h6-pawn was captured with a check.
White would have to move the rook.
Black defended against some discovered checks, but the position was pretty hopeless at this point.
Yes, this is a fork.
White trades off the defender.
White kept their light-squared on the strong diagonal.
After Qg6+, Grigory Levenfish resigned.
Only one legal move.
White was able to gain space in the center!
White brought their knight out.
Black captures.
White brought his queen to h3, eyeing the h7-pawn.
Black retreated and broke the pin.
White developed their light-squared bishop to a nice diagonal.

Black responded with a Counter-Desperado move!  

Getting rid of White's central pawn, and arguing that White's knight on b5 is hanging.
White captured the knight on e5, which was the only good move here.
Good game!
Black declined the gambit by supporting the d5-pawn.

The rook would fall, and with all of the checkmate threats, the game would end shortly. 

A very nice game, where Capablanca showcased how a few inaccuracies could lead to big advantages. 

Utilizing a desperado tactic in the beginning and an in-between move towards the end allowed for a very powerful attack on Black's king.
Black recaptured with the g-pawn.
Black recaptured.
The only move that didn't lose on the spot.

Instead of retreating the bishop, Capablanca added an attacker to the f6-knight, leaving the bishop hanging!

This was a masterful in-between move!
Let's see what would follow...
Black castled.
This doesn't work.

White occupied the c-file with their f-rook. 

The a-rook is keeping protection over the a-pawn.
Black developed their other knight.
...Rb8 was possibly a better choice — preparing ...Bb7.

Another great move occured! 

Simply threatening Qg6#.

The h6-pawn falls. 

It is impossible to defend the h7-square!

White traded their bishop for the knight, an important defender.

This also wins.
Checkmate.
Black moves away.
A discovered check, freeing up the h7-square.

Black responded in the same manner. 

We can see a Semi-Slav Opening position on the board.
This is check.

And now comes Nc6, forking the bishop on e7 and the rook on a5. 

Black cannot save both!

The engine says White has an advantage. Still, it isn't crushing.
...but Black gains the initiative by giving a check on b4, forcing White's king to move, otherwise, the d3-bishop would be hanging.
And this is over.
A classic pattern emerges.
Black moved to g7.
Black recaptures.
White went for the trade.
Let's look at the obvious continuation.
White simply recaptured.
Once again, a checkmate is unavoidable.
Material is equal here. But now White's desperado doesn't work.
A knight trade with a check would follow.
After ...Kxh6, Qh4+ and Qh7# is next.
